title: How Do I Get an Internship in College With Zero Experience?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You open LinkedIn. Every internship posting requires 'previous experience.' You have none. You need experience to get an internship. You need an internship to get experience. This is the paradox. It's frustrating. It's also solvable. Here is how to break out of the cycle.

The Hidden Truth: Entry-Level Means Entry-Level

Many job postings list '1-2 years experience' as a wish list, not a requirement. Employers want someone who can start without hand-holding. But they will hire a promising student who shows potential.

Apply anyway. Ignore the experience requirement. The worst that happens is they say no. The best that happens is you get an interview. You have nothing to lose.

If you wait until you have 'enough experience,' you'll never apply. No one feels ready. Everyone fakes it a little. You should too.

Your Campus Job Is Legitimate Experience

Working at the campus library. Being a resident assistant. Serving as a research assistant for a professor. These count as experience. They show responsibility, reliability, and maturity.

Put them on your resume. Describe what you did. 'Managed library operations during evening hours.' 'Resolved conflicts between residents.' 'Analyzed data for a psychology study.' These are real skills.

If you haven't worked on campus, start now. Even 10 hours a week builds a resume. It also pays you money.

Volunteer Work Counts as Experience

Helped a local nonprofit with their social media. Tutored younger students. Organized a fundraising event. These are not 'just volunteering.' They are evidence of initiative and competence.

Frame them as experience. 'Created social media content reaching 5,000 people.' 'Improved student test scores by 15% through weekly tutoring.' 'Managed $10,000 fundraising campaign.'

If you have a semester before internship season, volunteer strategically. Pick something related to your field. Build skills. Build your resume.

Class Projects Are Portfolio Pieces

That marketing plan you wrote for class. That data analysis project. That website you built. These are not just homework. They are evidence of your abilities.

Add them to your resume under 'Projects.' Describe what you did and what tools you used. 'Developed a marketing plan for a local business, increasing their social media engagement by 30%.'

If the project was hypothetical, say so. 'Created a mock website for an e-commerce store using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.' Employers want to see skills, not just grades.

Start Small: Local Businesses and Startups

Large companies have formal internship programs. They get thousands of applicants. Your odds are lower. Local businesses and startups have fewer resources. They need help. They are more willing to take a chance on a student.

Email 20 local businesses in your field. 'I'm a student at X University. I'm looking for an internship. I can work 10-15 hours per week. I'm willing to do whatever needs to be done.'

Most will ignore you. A few will reply. One will say yes. That's all you need.

Cold Email Like a Pro

Don't send generic emails. Research the company. Find a specific person (not 'info@'). Write a short, specific email.

Subject: 'Internship inquiry — [Your Name], [University]'

Body: 'I'm a [year] studying [major] at [university]. I'm impressed by [specific project or value of their company]. I'm looking for an internship where I can contribute while learning. I've attached my resume. Would you be open to a 15-minute conversation?'

Attach your resume. Follow up in one week. Be polite. Don't be annoying. One follow-up is enough.

The first internship is the hardest. Once you have one, the second comes easier. Focus on getting any experience in your field. It doesn't have to be your dream company. It just has to be a start.
